questions.question: How does anxious attachment typically affect boundary-setting in romantic relationships?

options.text: It makes setting boundaries easier because you care deeply about the relationship
Saying 'no' feels like risking the relationship; you over-adapt to partner's needs while ignoring your own
You maintain overly rigid boundaries to protect yourself from intimacy
Attachment style has no impact on communication patterns in relationships

questions.explanation: Anxious attachment is characterized by high need for reassurance and heightened sensitivity to rejection. Boundary challenges include: saying 'no' feels like risking the relationship, over-adapting to partner's needs while ignoring your own, difficulty tolerating partner's need for space, and fear that limits = rejection. Research shows anxious attachment is associated with poorer conflict resolution and greater relationship anxiety. Differentiation work for anxious attachment includes practicing distress tolerance when partner needs space and communicating needs clearly rather than testing.

questions.question: Your partner needs more alone time to recharge. What is a differentiated (healthy interdependence) response?

options.text: Get upset and accuse them of not caring about you
Shut down emotionally and withdraw yourself in response
Acknowledge their need AND express your own feelings, then collaborate on what 'more alone time' looks like
Say 'okay' but seethe with resentment without communicating it

questions.explanation: Healthy interdependence response: 'I hear that you need more alone time to recharge, and I want to support that. I also notice I feel anxious when you pull back — that's my stuff to work on, but I wanted to share it. Can we talk about what 'more alone time' looks like so I don't fill in the blanks with worry?' You honor their need, own your feelings, and invite collaborative problem-solving. This maintains connection while respecting autonomy — the core of differentiation.

questions.question: What did the Lavner et al. (2016) longitudinal study find about communication skills and relationship satisfaction?

options.text: Communication skills are the primary predictor of long-term relationship satisfaction
Cross-sectionally, communication and satisfaction correlate, but longitudinally, the link is weak in both directions
Communication skills have no relationship to satisfaction at all
Only couples with excellent communication skills stay together long-term

questions.explanation: Lavner et al. (2016) followed diverse low-income newlyweds and found that while cross-sectional correlations between communication and satisfaction were significant, longitudinal associations were weak in both directions — satisfaction predicts communication as much as communication predicts satisfaction. This means communication skills improve the PROCESS of conflict (reduced escalation, increased clarity) but don't guarantee long-term happiness if deeper issues (values mismatch, trauma, attachment insecurity) are present. Teaching communication to struggling couples doesn't reliably produce sustained satisfaction.

questions.question: Which relationship myth makes differentiation harder?

options.text: Conflict is inevitable when two separate people share a life
If you really loved me, you'd know what I need without me asking (mind-reading expectation)
Attachment styles predict automatic relational responses
Communication skills improve conflict process but don't resolve all relationship difficulties

questions.explanation: The mind-reading expectation — 'if you really loved me, you'd know what I need without me asking' — is fusion (we're so connected we're one person). No one is a mind reader. Differentiated communication means: I'm responsible for expressing my needs clearly; you're responsible for listening and responding. Research shows couples who expect mind-reading report lower satisfaction, while explicit communication of needs predicts better conflict resolution.

questions.question: When should you seek couples therapy or individual therapy for relationship patterns?

options.text: Only when you're already planning to separate
When communication skills haven't worked despite repeated effort, attachment patterns feel overwhelming, or you're replicating family-of-origin patterns
Never — all relationship problems can be solved with better communication skills
When your partner agrees that you're the one who needs to change

questions.explanation: Seek couples therapy if: you've tried communication skills repeatedly but conflict still escalates, patterns feel stuck despite both people wanting change, trust has been violated, one or both partners have trauma/addiction/severe family dysfunction, or you're considering separation. Seek individual therapy if: attachment patterns feel overwhelming, you're replicating family-of-origin patterns in romantic relationships, you experience intense emotional dysregulation during conflict, you have trauma history affecting intimacy, or you struggle with differentiation across all relationships. Skills alone can't heal wounds requiring relational repair or trauma processing.
